summary: Jeff Atwood examines the growing transistor counts in GPUs compared to CPUs, noting that NVIDIA's 8800 GTX surpasses Intel's quad-core processor in complexity. He explores how GPUs have evolved into stream processors capable of handling floating-point intensive tasks beyond graphics, citing Stanford's Folding@Home project as a key example. The post discusses emerging technologies like Brook Language and CUDA that enable C-like programming on GPUs. Atwood concludes that while CPUs and GPUs serve different purposes, the massive performance gains possible with GPU computing will drive many computational problems to migrate from CPU to GPU.
key_points: GPUs have surpassed CPUs in transistor count, with NVIDIA's 8800 GTX containing 680 million transistors versus Intel's quad-core at 582 million
Dedicated task-specific hardware dramatically outperforms general-purpose CPUs - Deep Blue's 1997 hardware still evaluates chess moves 25x faster than 2006's best CPUs
GPUs have evolved into stream processors that can handle non-graphics floating-point intensive workloads at scale
New technologies like ATI's Stream API and NVIDIA's CUDA are making GPU programming accessible through C-like languages
The symbiotic relationship between CPU and GPU will see many computing problems migrate to GPU for order-of-magnitude performance gains
money_quotes: GPUs now exceed the complexity of modern CPUs in terms of absolute transistor count.
Dedicated, task-specific hardware is orders of magnitude faster than what you can achieve with a general purpose CPU.
Ten year old custom hardware is still 25 times faster than the best general purpose CPUs. Amazing.
Modern GPUs are just as fast as custom hardware, but due to economies of scale are many, many times cheaper than custom hardware.
I expect the relationship between CPU and GPU to largely be a symbiotic one: they're good at different things.
I also expect quite a few computing problems to make the jump from CPU to GPU in the next 5 years.
key_insight: The explosive growth in GPU transistor counts and the emergence of general-purpose GPU programming frameworks signals a coming shift where many computational problems will migrate from CPU to GPU for dramatic performance gains.
